See some all a lovely familiar faces again. So that makes makes me smile every time. Good role here. And like I said, we are in a collaboration session, which for the ones who are new, we will work together on each other's influence challenges. And this has multiple great outcomes. Of course, first of all, you generate ideas how to overcome your current influence challenges. Second, by working with people, not from your industry, you often see they have a much more broader or creative view, because they don't have the hurdles you in the way of thinking that you build once you are working in a certain area for a longer time. And third, it's great to connect with like-minded people, which can continuously keep on helping you improve your influence skills and solving influence challenges together. So that being said, let me dive into what we are going to do today. Last teaching session was about contrast and we dove deep in the contrast phenomenon. I shared several examples how you can apply it. I also showed that contrast is an amplifier of each principle. And that led to the famous quote of Dr. Greg Knight, saying, if you can't find contrast, you're not looking hard enough. Or in other words, contrast is always available. And one shortcut of creating ideas is it's not X, but it is. And that's already put things in contrast by embedding that into your language. That being said, at the end of last teaching session, I gave three things, sleuths of influence do to use contrast. They know contrast is always available. Contrast is an amplifier for each principle. And I see here is a grammar error. Contrast should be a default way of communication. So these three things, and we're going to work today on contrast and how to apply it. And I like you to use the influence process, which I will briefly explain in a moment. But today, we're only going to think about contrast for two reasons. One, by zooming in on one specific principle, we really going to think deep on three different cases today. One of yourself, two of your co participants here on generating specific and the in depth ideas on applying contrast. And the second reason is we know that people in the beginning often get more easier creative with the principles come up with applications than with contrast. So therefore we like to use this session solely think about how to apply contrast to the influence challenges you go to work on today together with your peers. And for those who are new, the influence process, I will briefly explain it and also as a refresher for people who joined the last collaboration session. This process is specifically designed by Dr. Chardini and Dr. Nidot to get more efficient, more effective outcomes in a way, a much more efficient time window. Because a lot of in business, there are a lot of brainstorm sessions, and they take up a lot of time, and often they don't produce very effective outcomes, or you won't even walk away with an action plan. We don't want that. So as a refresher or as an introduction, how this influence process work. You will go into breakout rooms and you are with three or four people in your breakout rooms in a minute. And the first thing you do you assign a time, timekeeper. And I will explain in a minute what the role of the timekeeper is, but assigning the timekeeper shouldn't take much longer than 10 seconds. You just select one or somebody volunteers for it. The second thing you will do in the breakout session, you define the order in which you're going to handle each other cases. So one person goes first, second, third and fourth, if there is a fourth person. That shouldn't take longer than 20 seconds. So the first 30 seconds is have a timekeeper in place, define the order of the challenge in which order you're going to discuss each other's challenges. Then each person gets maximum five minutes to share their influence challenge. So you share that influence challenge by answering, by preferable answering the five questions. What's your situation? Who's involved? What do you hope to accomplish? What barriers do you see to accomplish the desired result? And what ideas you already been considering? Because answering those five questions gives your fellow participants a more broader or more in depth overview of what actually is your influence challenge. So each person gets maximum five minutes. That doesn't mean if you're done in three minutes, you have to stay talking for two minutes. You can just continue. It's a maximum amount of time. And then we do something that normally never happens during a brainstorm session. We actually start using our brain and not our mouth. We start thinking. And we think today, only how can we use contrast for the influence challenges you're working on? And as we know, contrast is always available. So we should be able to come up with ideas. The thinking time, if you are with three persons in the breakout room is seven minutes. If you are with four, it's five minutes. So this is for the timekeepers. Step back five minutes max to share your ideas, thinking time. If you are with three people, seven minutes, you are with four people in your breakout group, five minutes thinking time. The sound level during thinking time is silence because thinking is an individual exercise. And then we can really think by not talking. So step back one person talks. Thinking time. Everybody is silent, really using their full brainpower and thinking is also not one minute thinking, six minutes staring out the window. Seven minutes really trying to crack your brain to come up with ideas how to use contrast for the influence challenges. And then we are going to listen to each idea. So the sound level is one person's talking. The other people are listening. That doesn't that also means not providing feedback yet. So we are very human nature as a drive. Oh, yeah, that's a good idea. Or yes, but I think differently. Don't do that during step three. Just listen to the ideas of other people. If you are with three people in your breakout session, each person get max two and a half minutes. If you are with four people in the breakout rooms, every person get maximum two minutes. Again, if one person shares all their ideas in one minute, that's fine as well. Then you continue as a maximum amount of time, not a minimum. So we listen to all the ideas. And then we create an action plan and the action plan we want to create is a list of bullet points or in other words, a list of ideas, preferable prioritized. Or implementing them so you can walk away after your breakout session with a set of ideas you can implement right away. And prioritize them on impact and feasibility how easy it is to implement them. Well, it's, I would say it's different for each person. And it's kind of just, you know, it's like learning another language, I think, you know, at what point do you all of a sudden just start thinking in that other language before, and you don't, you know, you just say things you don't have to think anymore. And I'd say that's different for everyone and it, you know, basically matters on how much importance you put on it.
